title: Neural network modeling of many-body super- and sub-radiant dynamics
authors: Gianluca Lagnese, Laurin Brunner, Lorenzo Rossi, Darrick Chang, Markus Schmitt, Zala Lenarčič
abstract: There is significant interest in exploring novel phenomena in quantum light-matter interfaces, which are driven by the combination of structured dissipation and long-range interactions that are typical in such systems. To this end, it is important to develop new general numerical simulation techniques, which can access large system sizes and are not based on semi-classical approaches. Here, we report the first application of neural quantum states to obtain the dissipative dynamics of light-matter-coupled systems beyond what is accessible with exact and tensor-network calculations. We specifically apply this method to simulate the many-body emission dynamics of approximately 40 atoms, arranged in dense arrays in one and two dimensions. These systems have been chosen because they can support prominent subradiant dynamics at late times and could be realized with cold atomic quantum simulators.
summary: This paper introduces a neural network-based approach to simulate the many-body dissipative dynamics of large atom arrays. By using ResNet and dCNN architectures, the authors successfully model super- and sub-radiant regimes in 1D and 2D systems beyond the limits of exact diagonalization. The work demonstrates that neural networks can efficiently capture the complex, long-range correlations characteristic of subradiant decay.
main_problem: The challenge of simulating many-body super- and sub-radiant dynamics in large-scale light-matter interfaces where traditional exact and tensor-network methods fail.
main_result: The first successful application of Neural Quantum States (NQS) to capture the dissipative dynamics of 1D and 2D atom arrays up to 40 atoms, demonstrating that dCNN architectures can efficiently capture long-range correlations with fewer parameters than ResNet.
method: Neural Quantum States (NQS) using ResNet and dilated CNN (dCNN) architectures, implemented via a POVM-based representation and evolved using the Time-Dependent Variational Principle (TDVP) and Monte Carlo sampling.
model_or_system: Ordered, densely packed 1D and 2D arrays of cold atoms in free space, modeled by an effective spin Hamiltonian with photon-mediated long-range dipolar interactions and a Lindblad master equation.
key_observables: Number of excited atoms (excitation density), connected spin-spin correlators, and cumulative TDVP error (KL divergence).
important_parameters: Inter-atom distance (d), decay rate (Gamma_0), network depth (d), number of features (f), and kernel size (k).
main_assumptions: The light field is integrated out to provide an effective spin description; the use of a tetrahedral POVM for density matrix representation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 and 2 show benchmarking of NQS against exact quantum trajectories and convergence studies in 1D and 2D; Figure 3 compares excitation density across system sizes; Figure 4 provides a schematic comparison of ResNet and dCNN architectures.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of simulating large-scale dissipative dynamics, describes the NQS/POVM methodology and neural architectures (ResNet vs dCNN), presents benchmarking results against exact and semi-classical methods, and concludes with efficiency comparisons and future directions.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful new numerical tool (NQS) for studying the long-time subradiant regimes and many-body correlations in light-matter interfaces, which are otherwise inaccessible to standard methods.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) and (b): benchmarking of NQS ResNet results against quantum trajectories (QT) for a linear array of L = 16 atoms for different architecture parameters and kernel size k = 4. In (a) we show the time evolution of the number of excited atoms ne, computed with the NQS and compared with the quantum trajectories prediction (dashed) and contrasted with 2nd (dotted) and 3rd (dot-dashed) cumulant expansion. In (b) we monitor the cumulative error of the TDVP algorithm R(t), Eq. (4). In the inset of (b) we show the difference between ResNet and QT results, divided by the system volume L. (c) and (d): convergence of time evolution with NQS method by varying network parameters for a linear...

Figure 2. (a) and (b): benchmarking of ResNet against quantum trajectories for a square lattice of V = 4 × 4 atoms for different architecture parameters, with kernels size fixed to 2 × 2. In (a) we show the time evolution of the number of excited atoms computed with the NQS compared with the quantum trajectories (black dashed) prediction and contrasted with the 2nd (dotted) 3rd (dot-dashed) cumulant expansion. In the inset of (b) we show the difference between ResNet and quantum trajectories results divided by the number of atoms V . In (b) we monitor the cumulative error of the TDVP algorithm R(t). (c) and (d): convergence of time evolution with the NQS method by varying network parameters...

Figure 3. Comparison of dynamics for density of excitation at different system sizes and different geometries. Only ex- act/best results for the given system size are shown. Late time dynamics correspond to a power law decay, fitted on largest system sizes.
Figure 4. Sketch of the two architectures discussed in the main text. Left: representation of the ResNet architecture. Right: representation of the dilated convolutional neural net- work (dCNN).

Figure 5. Comparison of connected correlations (5), calcu- lated by different approaches: quantum trajectories (QT), best NQS approximation from the main text (f = 12, d = 4, k = 4) and cumulant expansions (2nd and 3rd order) for L = 16 in 1D. Left column: Correlations C(ℓ, t) at different relative sites ℓ. Right column: Absolute difference of correla- tions calculated by NQS or cumulant expansion with respect to the quantum trajectories result.
